title: Whittled hazel spatula
subtitle: Long flat spatula in green hazel for cooking and baking
excerpt: A long flat cooking spatula in green hazel. Flat blade, straight handle, rounded tip. A beginner project that builds push-cut control on the thin flat blade section without the complexity of a bowl.
sourceNotes: Beeton's Book of Household Management on kitchen spatula forms. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III on kitchen treen.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven timber, still high in moisture. Carves more easily than seasoned wood under hand tools.
A short length of round or split timber, ready to be worked into a blank for carving.
The safe default cut: blade travelling away from the body, driven by the forearm, off the end of the workpiece into open air.
A short controlled cut pivoted by the off-hand thumb on the back of the blade. Used for fine shaping where blade-travel is small.
A short straight-bladed knife with a stout handle; the Swedish school-craft standard for whittling and spoon-carving.

content.text: A cooking spatula is a flat-bladed scraping and flipping tool. The hazel version is 28 cm long with a 10 cm blade and an 18 cm handle. Hazel carves cleanly
green

and has a smooth fine grain on the flat face; the push cut follows the grain well and produces a clean surface with minimal tearing. Hazel is a coppice species; the billets come from poles cut at 3 to 5 years in the coppice cycle, with even straight grain along the full length.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool slips. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood
Billet
: 29 cm long, 4 cm wide, 2 cm deep. A straight hazel pole, the bark stripped. Windfall hazel from hedgerows or coppice off-cuts are the usual source. Sycamore or ash are direct substitutes.
Tools
A
sloyd knife
; a leather strop; raw linseed oil. No axe needed for a flat spatula from a straight pole.
Method

Mark the spatula outline on the flat face: 18 cm handle narrowing from 2 cm at the shoulder to 1.5 cm at the end; 10 cm blade widening from 2 cm at the shoulder to 3 cm at the widest point, then rounding to a smooth broad tip. Mark the side profile too: handle and blade are on the same plane (this is a flat spatula, not a cranked one).
Work the handle with
push cuts
to its final rectangular cross-section: 15 mm wide, 8 mm thick. Then move to the blade section.

Thin the blade on both faces to 3 mm at the center and 1.5 mm at the tip. Work symmetrically: one push cut on the top face at the shoulder, then a matching push cut on the bottom face at the shoulder. Alternating faces keeps the blade centered and prevents one face becoming thinner than the other.
Round the blade tip with
thumb-pivot cuts
on both faces. The tip is wide enough (30 mm) to need several passes.
Taper the handle-to-blade transition at the shoulder. The blade widens from the handle; a gentle concave taper at the shoulder looks clean and reduces the abrupt step.
Leave to dry for 2 weeks before finishing.
Finishing
Sand through 120 and 180 grit along the grain on all faces. Apply raw linseed oil; cure 2 weeks before food contact.
Variations
Sycamore instead of hazel: slightly finer grain, paler finished surface.
Narrower blade for turning eggs: reduce the blade width to 2 cm, keeping the same length. The narrower blade slides under fried eggs without breaking the yolk.
Left-handed version: the flat spatula is symmetrical; no adjustment needed.
Care
Rinse under warm water, towel-dry. Never soak. Re-oil every 10 to 15 uses.

items.symptom: Blade cracks along the grain as it dries
items.cause: Blade thinned too much before drying
items.fix: Leave the blade at 4 mm minimum during the green-carving stage. Final thinning to 2 mm after drying.

items.symptom: Blade bows to one face during drying
items.cause: One face thinner than the other
items.fix: Check the blade thickness at the shoulder, the center, and 2 cm from the tip before setting aside. Adjust with light push cuts on the thick side.
